text_segment: Our solar system has eight planets orbiting the Sun. The four inner planets are rocky and small. The four outer planets are made mostly of gas and are much larger.
question: Describe the basic structure of our solar system.
Active: Eight planets orbit the Sun in our solar system.
Progressive: Planets are orbiting the Sun continuously.
Passive: The Sun is orbited by eight planets.
Subordinate: While inner planets are rocky, outer planets are gaseous.
order_active_beginner_1: eight planets,orbit the sun,in our,solar system,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: while,inner planets are rocky,,,outer planets,are gaseous,.

text_segment: The Sun is a star at the center of our solar system. It is a giant ball of hot gas that gives us light and heat. The Sun is about 93 million miles from Earth.
question: Explain what the Sun is.
Active: The Sun provides light and heat to Earth.
Progressive: The Sun is giving us energy every day.
Passive: Light and heat are provided by the Sun.
Subordinate: Because the Sun is a star, it produces its own light.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,the sun is a star,,,it produces,its own light,.

text_segment: The Sun is made mostly of hydrogen and helium gas. Nuclear reactions in the Sun's core turn hydrogen into helium. This process releases huge amounts of energy as light and heat.
question: Describe what the Sun is made of.
Active: Hydrogen and helium make up most of the Sun.
Progressive: Nuclear reactions are releasing energy in the Sun.
Passive: Energy is released by nuclear reactions in the Sun.
Subordinate: When hydrogen becomes helium, energy is released.
order_active_beginner_1: hydrogen and helium,make up,most of,the sun,.
order_passive_beginner_1: energy,is released,by nuclear reactions,in the sun,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: when,hydrogen becomes helium,,,energy,is released,.

text_segment: Sunspots are dark areas on the Sun's surface that are cooler than surrounding areas. They are caused by magnetic activity. Sunspots can affect radio signals and power grids on Earth.
question: Explain what sunspots are.
Active: Sunspots appear as dark cool spots on the Sun.
Progressive: Sunspots are affecting radio signals on Earth.
Passive: Radio signals are affected by sunspot activity.
Subordinate: Because sunspots have strong magnetism, they appear darker.
order_active_beginner_1: sunspots,appear as dark,cool spots,on the sun,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,sunspots have strong magnetism,,,they appear,darker,.

text_segment: The four inner planets are Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars. They are called rocky planets because they have solid surfaces. These planets are closest to the Sun.
question: Describe the inner planets of our solar system.
Active: Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars orbit closest to the Sun.
Progressive: The inner planets are orbiting near the Sun.
Passive: Solid surfaces are found on the inner planets.
Subordinate: Because they have rocky surfaces, they are called terrestrial planets.
order_active_beginner_1: mercury venus earth mars,orbit closest,to the,sun,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,they have rocky surfaces,,,they are called,terrestrial planets,.

text_segment: The four outer planets are Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune. They are called gas giants because they are made mostly of gas. Jupiter is the largest planet in our solar system.
question: Explain what the outer planets are like.
Active: Gas giants are made mostly of hydrogen and helium.
Progressive: Jupiter is spinning faster than any other planet.
Passive: The outer planets are called gas giants.
Subordinate: Because they lack solid surfaces, they are called gas giants.
order_active_beginner_1: gas giants,are made mostly,of hydrogen,and helium,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,they lack solid surfaces,,,they are called,gas giants,.

text_segment: Five planets can be seen without a telescope: Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn. Venus is the brightest planet in our sky. Mars appears reddish because of iron in its soil.
question: Describe which planets we can see without a telescope.
Active: Five planets shine brightly enough to see unaided.
Progressive: Venus is shining as the brightest planet tonight.
Passive: Five planets can be seen without telescopes.
Subordinate: Because Mars has iron in its soil, it looks red.
tiles_subordinate: mars has iron in its soil
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,mars has iron in its soil,,,it looks,red,.

text_segment: Mercury and Venus can show phases like the Moon. This happens because they orbit between Earth and the Sun. The outer planets always look fully lit from Earth.
question: Explain why some planets show phases.
Active: Mercury and Venus show phases like the Moon.
Progressive: Venus is showing a crescent phase this month.
Passive: Phases are shown by planets closer to the Sun.
Subordinate: Because they orbit inside Earth's orbit, they show phases.
order_passive_beginner_1: phases,are shown,by planets closer,to the sun,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,they orbit inside earth's orbit,,,they show,phases,.

text_segment: Planets appear to move across the sky over weeks and months. They travel along a path called the ecliptic near the zodiac constellations. Sometimes planets seem to move backward, which is called retrograde motion.
question: Describe how planets move across the sky.
Active: Planets travel along a path called the ecliptic.
Progressive: Mars is moving through the constellation Leo.
Passive: The ecliptic is followed by all the planets.
Subordinate: When planets appear to move backward, it is called retrograde.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: when,planets appear to move backward,,,it is called,retrograde,.

text_segment: The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite. It orbits Earth about once every 27 days. The Moon has no atmosphere, so there is no weather or sound on its surface.
question: Describe basic facts about the Moon.
Active: The Moon orbits Earth once every 27 days.
Progressive: The Moon is orbiting Earth right now.
Passive: Earth is orbited by one natural satellite.
Subordinate: Because the Moon has no atmosphere, there is no weather.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,the moon has no atmosphere,,,there is,no weather,.

text_segment: The Moon appears to change shape during the month. These changes are called phases. The phases happen because we see different amounts of the Moon's sunlit side as it orbits Earth.
question: Explain why the Moon has phases.
Active: The Moon's phases show different amounts of sunlight.
Progressive: The Moon is changing from crescent to quarter phase.
Passive: Different amounts of sunlight are seen during phases.
Subordinate: As the Moon orbits Earth, its phases change.
order_progressive_beginner_1: the moon,is changing,from crescent to,quarter phase,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: as,the moon orbits earth,,,its phases,change,.

text_segment: The Moon's surface has dark flat areas called maria or seas. It also has many craters made by meteor impacts. The largest crater visible from Earth is called Tycho.
question: Describe the features on the Moon's surface.
Active: Craters and maria cover the Moon's surface.
Progressive: Scientists are studying the Moon's many craters.
Passive: The Moon's surface is covered with craters.
Subordinate: When meteors struck the Moon, they created craters.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: when,meteors struck the moon,,,they created,craters,.

text_segment: Gravity keeps the Moon in orbit around Earth. The Moon's gravity also pulls on Earth, causing ocean tides. The same side of the Moon always faces Earth.
question: Explain what keeps the Moon in orbit.
Active: Earth's gravity keeps the Moon in orbit.
Progressive: The Moon is pulling on Earth's oceans.
Passive: Ocean tides are caused by the Moon's gravity.
Subordinate: Because gravity pulls on the Moon, it stays in orbit.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,gravity pulls on the moon,,,it stays,in orbit,.

text_segment: A lunar eclipse happens when Earth passes between the Sun and Moon. Earth's shadow falls on the Moon, making it look dark or reddish. Lunar eclipses only happen during a full Moon.
question: Explain what causes a lunar eclipse.
Active: Earth's shadow causes lunar eclipses.
Progressive: Earth's shadow is covering the Moon tonight.
Passive: The Moon is darkened by Earth's shadow.
Subordinate: When Earth blocks the Sun's light, a lunar eclipse occurs.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: when,earth blocks the sun's light,,,a lunar eclipse,occurs,.

text_segment: A solar eclipse happens when the Moon passes between Earth and the Sun. The Moon blocks the Sun's light, making day look like night. Never look directly at a solar eclipse without special glasses.
question: Describe what causes a solar eclipse.
Active: The Moon blocks the Sun during a solar eclipse.
Progressive: The Moon is blocking sunlight during the eclipse.
Passive: Sunlight is blocked by the Moon during an eclipse.
Subordinate: Because the Moon blocks the Sun, day becomes dark.
order_active_beginner_1: the moon,blocks the sun,during a,solar eclipse,.
order_passive_beginner_1: sunlight,is blocked,by the moon,during an eclipse,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,the moon blocks the sun,,,day becomes,dark,.

text_segment: Stars are huge balls of hot gas that produce their own light. Our Sun is a medium-sized yellow star. Stars come in different sizes and colors depending on their temperature.
question: Describe what stars are.
Active: Stars produce their own light from hot gas.
Progressive: Stars are shining across the night sky.
Passive: Light is produced by stars through nuclear fusion.
Subordinate: Because stars are extremely hot, they glow brightly.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,stars are extremely hot,,,they glow,brightly,.

text_segment: A star's color tells us its temperature. Blue stars are the hottest, then white, yellow, orange, and red. Red stars are the coolest. Our yellow Sun has a medium temperature.
question: Explain what star colors mean.
Active: Star colors indicate their surface temperature.
Progressive: Blue stars are burning much hotter than red stars.
Passive: Temperature is indicated by a star's color.
Subordinate: Because blue stars are hottest, they shine blue-white.
order_progressive_beginner_1: blue stars,are burning,much hotter than,red stars,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,blue stars are hottest,,,they shine,blue-white,.

text_segment: Star brightness is measured in magnitude. Lower numbers mean brighter stars. The brightest stars have magnitude 1 or less. Sirius is the brightest star in our night sky.
question: Describe how star brightness is measured.
Active: Astronomers measure star brightness using magnitude.
Progressive: Sirius is shining as the brightest star tonight.
Passive: Star brightness is measured by magnitude numbers.
Subordinate: When magnitude numbers are lower, stars are brighter.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: when,magnitude numbers are lower,,,stars are,brighter,.

text_segment: Constellations are patterns of stars that people have named. Ancient people saw shapes like animals and heroes in the stars. There are 88 official constellations recognized today.
question: Explain what constellations are.
Active: Constellations form patterns of stars in the sky.
Progressive: People are identifying constellations in the night sky.
Passive: Star patterns are called constellations.
Subordinate: When stars form recognizable shapes, we call them constellations.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: when,stars form recognizable shapes,,,we call them,constellations,.

text_segment: The Big Dipper is one of the easiest star patterns to find. It looks like a giant ladle or cup with a handle. Two stars in the Big Dipper point toward Polaris, the North Star.
question: Describe the Big Dipper and how to find it.
Active: The Big Dipper points toward the North Star.
Progressive: The Big Dipper is rotating around Polaris.
Passive: Polaris is found using the Big Dipper's pointer stars.
Subordinate: If you follow the pointer stars, you find Polaris.
order_passive_beginner_1: polaris,is found,using the big dipper's,pointer stars,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: if,you follow the pointer stars,,,you find,polaris,.

text_segment: The zodiac is a band of 12 constellations along the ecliptic. The Sun, Moon, and planets appear to travel through these constellations. Examples include Leo, Scorpius, and Gemini.
question: Explain what the zodiac constellations are.
Active: Zodiac constellations lie along the ecliptic path.
Progressive: The Sun is moving through zodiac constellations.
Passive: The ecliptic is marked by zodiac constellations.
Subordinate: Because planets follow the ecliptic, they pass through zodiac signs.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,planets follow the ecliptic,,,they pass through,zodiac signs,.

text_segment: Polaris is called the North Star because it stays almost directly above Earth's North Pole. All other stars appear to rotate around it during the night. Sailors used Polaris for navigation.
question: Describe why Polaris is special.
Active: Polaris stays fixed above the North Pole.
Progressive: Stars are rotating around Polaris all night.
Passive: Navigation was aided by using Polaris.
Subordinate: Because Polaris marks north, it helps with navigation.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,polaris marks north,,,it helps with,navigation,.

text_segment: The Milky Way is our home galaxy. It contains billions of stars including our Sun. From Earth, it looks like a faint glowing band across the night sky.
question: Explain what we see when we look at the Milky Way.
Active: The Milky Way contains billions of stars.
Progressive: We are looking at our own galaxy's edge.
Passive: Billions of stars are contained in the Milky Way.
Subordinate: When we see the Milky Way, we look through our galaxy.
order_passive_beginner_1: billions of stars,are contained,in the,milky way,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: when,we see the milky way,,,we look through,our galaxy,.

text_segment: Telescopes collect light to make distant objects look bigger and brighter. There are two main types: refractors use lenses and reflectors use mirrors. Bigger telescopes can see fainter objects.
question: Describe how telescopes help astronomers.
Active: Telescopes collect light and magnify distant objects.
Progressive: Astronomers are using telescopes to study stars.
Passive: Light is collected and magnified by telescopes.
Subordinate: Because telescopes gather more light, we see fainter objects.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,telescopes gather more light,,,we see,fainter objects,.

text_segment: Binoculars are a great tool for beginning astronomers. They show craters on the Moon and Jupiter's moons. Binoculars are easier to use than telescopes and show a wider view of the sky.
question: Explain why binoculars are useful for astronomy.
Active: Binoculars reveal Moon craters and Jupiter's moons.
Progressive: Astronomers are viewing the Moon through binoculars.
Passive: Moon craters are revealed by using binoculars.
Subordinate: Because binoculars show a wide view, they help find objects.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,binoculars show a wide view,,,they help,find objects,.

text_segment: Radio telescopes detect radio waves from space instead of visible light. They can observe objects hidden by dust clouds. The largest radio telescope dishes are hundreds of feet wide.
question: Describe what radio telescopes do.
Active: Radio telescopes detect radio waves from space.
Progressive: Radio telescopes are receiving signals from distant galaxies.
Passive: Radio waves from space are detected by special telescopes.
Subordinate: Because radio waves pass through dust, they reveal hidden objects.
order_passive_beginner_1: radio waves,from space,are detected,by special telescopes,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,radio waves pass through dust,,,they reveal,hidden objects,.

text_segment: Light pollution is unwanted artificial light that brightens the night sky. City lights make it hard to see faint stars. Dark sky locations away from cities are best for stargazing.
question: Explain how light pollution affects astronomy.
Active: Light pollution hides faint stars from view.
Progressive: City lights are blocking our view of stars.
Passive: Faint stars are hidden by light pollution.
Subordinate: When cities are bright, fewer stars can be seen.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: when,cities are bright,,,fewer stars,can be seen,.

text_segment: Never look directly at the Sun without special solar filters. The Sun can damage your eyes in seconds. Use eclipse glasses or a pinhole projector to view the Sun safely.
question: Describe how to safely observe the Sun.
Active: Special filters protect your eyes from the Sun.
Progressive: Astronomers are using solar filters safely.
Passive: Eyes are protected by special solar filters.
Subordinate: Because sunlight damages eyes, use special filters.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,sunlight damages eyes,,,use special,filters,.

text_segment: Dress warmly when observing at night because temperatures drop after sunset. Bring a red flashlight to see your star charts without ruining your night vision. Allow 20 minutes for your eyes to adjust to darkness.
question: Explain how to prepare for night sky observing.
Active: Red flashlights preserve your night vision.
Progressive: Your eyes are adjusting to the darkness.
Passive: Night vision is preserved by using red lights.
Subordinate: After 20 minutes in darkness, your eyes adjust fully.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: after,20 minutes in darkness,,,your eyes,adjust fully,.

text_segment: Meteors are streaks of light caused by space rocks burning up in Earth's atmosphere. People call them shooting stars. During meteor showers, you might see dozens of meteors per hour.
question: Describe what meteors are.
Active: Meteors create bright streaks across the sky.
Progressive: Space rocks are burning up in our atmosphere.
Passive: Bright streaks are created by burning meteors.
Subordinate: When space rocks hit the atmosphere, they become meteors.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: when,space rocks hit the atmosphere,,,they become,meteors,.

text_segment: Comets are icy objects that orbit the Sun. When a comet gets close to the Sun, ice turns to gas and forms a glowing tail. Famous comets like Halley's Comet return every few decades.
question: Describe what comets are.
Active: Comets develop glowing tails near the Sun.
Progressive: The comet is growing a bright tail.
Passive: Glowing tails are formed by comets near the Sun.
Subordinate: When comets approach the Sun, they develop tails.
order_passive_beginner_1: glowing tails,are formed,by comets,near the sun,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: when,comets approach the sun,,,they develop,tails,.

text_segment: Asteroids are rocky objects that orbit the Sun. Most asteroids are found in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter. Some asteroids are tiny rocks while others are hundreds of miles wide.
question: Explain what asteroids are.
Active: Asteroids orbit the Sun between Mars and Jupiter.
Progressive: Scientists are tracking asteroids near Earth.
Passive: The asteroid belt is found between Mars and Jupiter.
Subordinate: Because asteroids are rocky, they differ from icy comets.
order_passive_beginner_1: the asteroid belt,is found,between mars,and jupiter,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,asteroids are rocky,,,they differ from,icy comets,.

text_segment: Artificial satellites orbit Earth and can be seen crossing the night sky. The International Space Station is the brightest satellite. It looks like a steady moving star.

Active: Satellites look like moving stars crossing the sky.
Progressive: The space station is passing overhead tonight.
Passive: Satellites can be seen moving across the night sky.
Subordinate: If you watch carefully, satellites cross the sky steadily.
order_passive_beginner_1: satellites,can be seen moving,across the,night sky,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: if,you watch carefully,,,satellites cross,the sky steadily,.

text_segment: Space telescopes orbit above Earth's atmosphere. They get clearer views because air does not blur the images. The Hubble and James Webb telescopes have taken amazing pictures of distant galaxies.
question: Explain why space telescopes are useful.
Active: Space telescopes see more clearly above the atmosphere.
Progressive: The Webb telescope is photographing distant galaxies.
Passive: Clearer images are captured by space telescopes.
Subordinate: Because they are above the atmosphere, images are clearer.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,they are above the atmosphere,,,images are,clearer,.

text_segment: A planetarium is a building with a domed ceiling where stars and planets are projected. Shows teach visitors about the night sky. Planetariums help people learn constellations and astronomy.
question: Describe what you can learn at a planetarium.
Active: Planetariums teach visitors about stars and planets.
Progressive: The planetarium is showing the summer constellations.
Passive: Stars and planets are projected onto the dome.
Subordinate: When you visit a planetarium, you learn about the sky.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: when,you visit a planetarium,,,you learn about,the sky,.

text_segment: A star party is an event where people gather to observe the night sky together. Experienced astronomers share their telescopes with beginners. Star parties are a fun way to learn astronomy.
question: Describe what happens at a star party.
Active: Astronomers share telescopes at star parties.
Progressive: People are viewing Saturn's rings at the party.
Passive: Telescopes are shared with beginners at star parties.
Subordinate: When you attend a star party, you can see through telescopes.

text_segment: Star charts are maps of the night sky. They show where to find constellations and planets. Apps on phones can also help you identify what you see in the sky.
question: Explain how star charts help astronomers.
Active: Star charts show the locations of constellations.
Progressive: Astronomers are using star charts to find objects.
Passive: Constellations are located using star charts.
Subordinate: By using star charts, you can find any constellation.

text_segment: Galaxies are huge collections of stars, gas, and dust held together by gravity. The Milky Way is a spiral galaxy. There are billions of galaxies in the universe.
question: Describe what galaxies are.
Active: Galaxies contain billions of stars held by gravity.
Progressive: Telescopes are revealing galaxies billions of light-years away.
Passive: Billions of stars are contained within galaxies.
Subordinate: Because gravity holds them together, galaxies keep their shape.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,gravity holds them together,,,galaxies keep,their shape,.

text_segment: A light-year is the distance light travels in one year. Stars are so far away that their light takes years to reach us. The nearest star beyond the Sun is over four light-years away.
question: Explain what a light-year measures.
Active: Light-years measure vast distances in space.
Progressive: Starlight is traveling through space for years.
Passive: Distance in space is measured in light-years.
Subordinate: Because stars are far away, their light takes years to arrive.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,stars are far away,,,their light takes,years to arrive,.

text_segment: Astronomers study stars, planets, and galaxies. They use telescopes and computers to make discoveries. Other space careers include aerospace engineers, planetarium educators, and spacecraft designers.

Active: Astronomers study the universe using powerful telescopes.
Progressive: Scientists are discovering new planets around distant stars.
Passive: The universe is studied by professional astronomers.
Subordinate: If you love space, astronomy offers exciting careers.
